items.q: What actually shortens during muscle contraction?
items.correct: The actin and myosin filaments slide past each other, shortening the sarcomere; the filaments themselves do not shorten.
items.misconception: The actin and myosin filaments themselves get shorter.
items.random_wrong: The muscle cells split in half to shorten the muscle.
items.why: Students assume the filaments contract rather than sliding past one another.

items.correct: No - lactate is produced in anaerobic respiration when oxygen is short, and is later oxidised when oxygen returns.
items.misconception: Lactic acid is produced because too much oxygen reaches the muscle.
items.random_wrong: Lactic acid is produced only in the liver, never in muscle.
items.why: Students reverse the oxygen relationship, thinking excess oxygen causes lactate.

items.q: Is ATP a long-term energy store for muscles?
items.correct: No - ATP is the immediate energy currency and is recycled constantly; muscles store energy as glycogen.
items.misconception: Muscles store large amounts of ATP ready for exercise.
items.random_wrong: ATP is made only in the blood and delivered to muscle.
items.why: Students treat ATP as a stockpiled fuel rather than a rapidly recycled carrier.

items.q: The immediate energy source for muscle contraction is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: ATP. “glucose” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Anaerobic respiration in human muscle produces:
items.why: Correct answer: lactate. “ethanol and carbon dioxide” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: After sprinting, the oxygen debt is repaid in order to:
items.why: It's oxidise the lactate that accumulated. “rebuild the broken sarcomeres” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
